content.text: DSPy (Declarative Self-improving Python) is a framework that treats prompts as learnable parameters. Instead of hand-crafting the exact wording of instructions, you declare what a module should compute — its input fields and output fields — and let a compilation step discover the best prompts and few-shot examples automatically. The mental model is close to machine learning: you provide a labeled dataset, define a metric function that scores outputs, and an optimizer searches the space of prompts on your behalf.

At its core DSPy separates two concerns that manual prompt engineering conflates: program structure and prompt parameters. Program structure is expressed in Python: you define Signatures that name the inputs and outputs of a reasoning step, and you compose Modules such as

just as you would compose functions. Prompt parameters — the exact instruction wording, the choice of few-shot demonstrations — are left unspecified until compilation time. The compiler fills them in by running your program against training examples, evaluating results with your metric, and selecting the parameter settings that maximize the score.
This design means that if you swap the underlying LLM, change the task specification, or add more training data, you simply recompile rather than rewrite prompts by hand.

Manual prompt engineering has two persistent problems. First, it does not scale: as pipelines grow to contain five or ten chained steps, the combinatorial space of prompt variants becomes unmanageable. Improving one step often degrades another, and there is no principled way to find the global optimum. Second, prompts degrade silently: they were tuned against a mental model of the LLM's behaviour that changes with every model update. DSPy externalises this optimisation into a repeatable, measurable process.

The deeper point is epistemological. A hand-crafted prompt embeds tacit knowledge about what the LLM responds to well. DSPy makes that knowledge explicit, testable, and version-controlled. You can retrain when the model changes, audit what examples the optimizer chose, and compare two compiled programs objectively using the same metric.
the compilation process is deterministic given the same dataset, metric, and random seed, so prompt improvements can be tracked like model checkpoints.
modules are ordinary Python objects; you can chain, branch, and reuse them without reasoning about prompt interaction effects — the optimizer handles that.
recompile with a different LLM and the optimizer finds prompts that suit the new model's behaviour.
every compilation run produces a numeric score on the dev set, giving an objective criterion for whether a change helped.
once the dataset and metric exist, iterating on the program architecture is faster than iterating on prompt text.
the optimizer picks which training examples to include as demonstrations based on what actually improves the metric, not intuition.
even a small dataset of 20–50 examples with correct outputs is needed. If ground truth is expensive to produce, DSPy cannot help until you invest in labeling.
a poorly designed metric misleads the optimizer. If the metric does not correlate with human judgment, the compiled program will be optimized in the wrong direction.
optimizers like MIPRO make hundreds of LLM calls during compilation. This is a one-time or infrequent cost, but it requires a budget.
when the compiled program underperforms, the failure could be in the program structure, the metric, the dataset, or the optimizer's configuration. Diagnosing requires examining all three.
teams already expert in prompt engineering may find the compilation mental model adds friction for simple single-step tasks.

The compilation process has four stages. First, the optimizer samples a batch of training examples and runs your DSPy program forward, collecting the LLM's inputs and outputs at every module. Second, it evaluates each output using your metric function. Third, it uses those scores to select candidate few-shot demonstrations from the training set — examples where the model succeeded with a particular chain of reasoning. Fourth, for optimizers that also tune instructions (such as MIPRO), it proposes new instruction variants and evaluates them. The result is a compiled program where each Predict or ChainOfThought module carries a frozen set of few-shot demonstrations and, optionally, a refined instruction string.

declares the interface of a reasoning step as a string of the form `input_field -> output_field` or as a typed class. It does not specify how the LLM should reason — only what goes in and what comes out. A
wraps a Signature with a prompting strategy: Predict sends the Signature directly; ChainOfThought asks for a rationale before the answer; ReAct interleaves tool calls with reasoning steps. An
(historically called a Teleprompter) takes a program, a training dataset, and a metric, and returns a compiled program. A
is any Python callable that takes (gold_label, prediction, trace) and returns a float or boolean indicating quality.

attrs.alt: DSPy compilation loop: Program (Signatures + Modules) feeds into Training Examples and Metric Function, both flow into the Optimizer/Teleprompter, which produces an Optimized Program with baked-in few-shot demos and tuned instructions.

content.text: Runs the program forward, collects trajectories that pass the metric, and uses them as few-shot demos for each module.
Best starting point; requires only ~20 training examples; fast.
Runs BootstrapFewShot multiple times with different random seeds and selects the best compiled program.
When you have a budget for 5–20 compilation runs; small to medium datasets.
Multi-stage optimizer that proposes instruction variants via a meta-LLM and evaluates them with Bayesian optimization.
When instruction wording matters as much as few-shot selection; requires 50+ examples.
Treats instruction candidates as arms in a Bayesian bandit and allocates evaluation budget to the most promising ones.
Large programs with many modules where exhaustive search is too expensive.
Uses bootstrapped demonstrations to fine-tune the underlying LLM weights rather than just optimizing prompts.
When prompt optimization has plateaued and you have enough data for fine-tuning.
You have a multi-step pipeline where manual prompt tuning across steps is intractable.
You can define a measurable metric for task quality (exact match, F1, custom LLM judge).
You have at least 20 labeled examples; 50–200 is better for MIPRO.
You need to migrate a working pipeline to a new LLM and want to re-optimize systematically.
You are running evaluations and want to separate pipeline architecture decisions from prompt-wording decisions.
Prompt regressions are causing reliability problems and you want a reproducible optimization process.

import dspy

# Configure the language model
lm = dspy.LM("openai/gpt-4o-mini", max_tokens=512)
dspy.configure(lm=lm)

# A Signature declares inputs and outputs.
# The docstring becomes the instruction in the prompt.
class SentimentClassifier(dspy.Signature):
    """Classify the sentiment of a product review."""
    review: str = dspy.InputField(desc="The product review text")
    sentiment: str = dspy.OutputField(desc="positive, negative, or neutral")

# A Module wraps the Signature with a prompting strategy.
class Classifier(dspy.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.classify = dspy.Predict(SentimentClassifier)

    def forward(self, review: str) -> dspy.Prediction:
        return self.classify(review=review)

# Use it directly (before compilation):
clf = Classifier()
result = clf(review="Battery life is outstanding, arrived on time.")
print(result.sentiment)  # "positive"

from dspy.teleprompt import BootstrapFewShot
import dspy

# --- 1. Prepare training data ---
trainset = [
    dspy.Example(review="Works perfectly, highly recommend!", sentiment="positive").with_inputs("review"),
    dspy.Example(review="Broke after one week, terrible quality.", sentiment="negative").with_inputs("review"),
    dspy.Example(review="Average product, does what it says.", sentiment="neutral").with_inputs("review"),
    # ... 20–50 such examples in practice
]

# --- 2. Define a metric ---
def sentiment_metric(gold: dspy.Example, pred: dspy.Prediction, trace=None) -> bool:
    """Return True if the predicted sentiment matches the gold label."""
    return gold.sentiment.lower() == pred.sentiment.strip().lower()

# --- 3. Compile ---
optimizer = BootstrapFewShot(metric=sentiment_metric, max_bootstrapped_demos=4)
compiled_clf = optimizer.compile(Classifier(), trainset=trainset)

# --- 4. Evaluate on a held-out dev set ---
from dspy.evaluate import Evaluate

devset = [...]  # your validation examples
evaluator = Evaluate(devset=devset, metric=sentiment_metric, num_threads=4)
score = evaluator(compiled_clf)
print(f"Dev accuracy: {score:.1%}")

# --- 5. Inspect what the optimizer chose ---
print(compiled_clf.classify.demos)  # the few-shot examples baked into the prompt

import dspy
from dspy.retrieve import ColBERTv2

# --- Signatures ---
class GenerateSearchQuery(dspy.Signature):
    """Generate a precise search query to retrieve relevant passages."""
    question: str = dspy.InputField()
    query: str = dspy.OutputField(desc="a focused retrieval query")

class SynthesizeAnswer(dspy.Signature):
    """Given retrieved context passages, answer the question accurately."""
    question: str = dspy.InputField()
    context: list[str] = dspy.InputField(desc="retrieved passages")
    answer: str = dspy.OutputField(desc="a faithful, concise answer")

# --- Program ---
class RAGPipeline(dspy.Module):
    def __init__(self, retriever, k=3):
        super().__init__()
        self.gen_query = dspy.ChainOfThought(GenerateSearchQuery)
        self.retrieve = retriever
        self.synthesize = dspy.ChainOfThought(SynthesizeAnswer)
        self.k = k

    def forward(self, question: str) -> dspy.Prediction:
        query_pred = self.gen_query(question=question)
        passages = self.retrieve(query_pred.query, k=self.k).passages
        answer_pred = self.synthesize(question=question, context=passages)
        return dspy.Prediction(answer=answer_pred.answer, context=passages)

# --- Metric: answer must be faithful to retrieved context ---
def faithfulness_metric(gold, pred, trace=None):
    if not pred.answer or not pred.context:
        return 0.0
    judge = dspy.Predict("answer, context -> is_faithful: bool")
    result = judge(answer=pred.answer, context=str(pred.context))
    return float(result.is_faithful)

# --- Compile ---
retriever = ColBERTv2(url="http://localhost:8893/api/search")
rag = RAGPipeline(retriever=retriever)

from dspy.teleprompt import BootstrapFewShotWithRandomSearch
optimizer = BootstrapFewShotWithRandomSearch(
    metric=faithfulness_metric,
    max_bootstrapped_demos=3,
    num_candidate_programs=8,
)
compiled_rag = optimizer.compile(rag, trainset=trainset)
compiled_rag.save("rag_compiled.json")

A legal document review team at a mid-sized firm used DSPy to build a pipeline that extracts contract clauses and classifies their risk level. Initially, a senior lawyer spent two days hand-crafting prompts for each of five steps. When they switched to DSPy with a 60-example labeled dataset and a metric that checked clause category against a paralegal's labels, the compiled program outperformed the hand-written version by 11 percentage points on a 200-document test set. More importantly, when the team upgraded from GPT-3.5-Turbo to GPT-4o-mini three months later, they recompiled in two hours rather than rewriting prompts over several days.
a metric that is too lenient lets the optimizer plateau at the first set of demos it tries. Make sure the metric rejects at least 20–30% of outputs on the training set initially.
with fewer than 15 examples the bootstrapped demonstrations are not diverse enough to generalize. Aim for 50+ for MIPRO.
evaluating the compiled program on the same data used for compilation gives an inflated score. Always hold out a separate dev set.
DSPy records intermediate LLM calls in the trace. Inspecting failing traces reveals whether the problem is in the query generation step, the retrieval step, or the synthesis step.
always inspect the baked-in demonstrations to verify they are sensible. The optimizer can bootstrap misleading examples if the metric has gaps.
compilation is expensive. Save the compiled program with `program.save()` and reload it at inference time.
Start with BootstrapFewShot to establish a baseline before spending compute on MIPRO.
Write your metric before writing your program — a clear metric forces clarity about task definition.
Keep Signatures narrowly scoped; broad Signatures with many output fields produce high-variance outputs.
Use `dspy.inspect_history()` during development to see exactly what prompts are being sent to the LLM.
Store the compiled program JSON in version control alongside the dataset and metric used to produce it.
Maintain a regression suite: after recompiling, verify that previously correct examples are still handled correctly.
Use thread-based evaluation (`num_threads > 1`) to speed up compilation; LLM calls are the bottleneck.

Compilation time scales with the number of training examples, the number of modules in the program, and the optimizer's search budget. BootstrapFewShot on a 50-example dataset with a 3-module program typically requires 150–300 LLM calls. MIPRO with `num_candidates=20` and 100 training examples can require 2,000–5,000 LLM calls. In practice, batch these calls in parallel by setting `num_threads` in the Evaluate call and by running compilation on a machine with a high API rate limit. Inference-time cost is unchanged from a manually prompted program — the compiled program is just a program with frozen parameters, not a different kind of LLM call.

For large-scale deployments, compile once, save the JSON artifact, and load it in production workers. The compiled program is fully serializable and does not require the optimizer or training data at inference time.
the core library; install with `pip install dspy-ai`. Supports OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, local models via Ollama, and any LiteLLM-compatible endpoint.
a community tool that renders the trace graph of a compiled program, useful for debugging multi-step pipelines.
log compilation runs, metric scores, and compiled program artifacts for experiment tracking.
can be attached to DSPy via a LangChain integration to log and trace every LLM call during compilation and inference.
retrieval backends for RAG pipelines built with DSPy; DSPy ships native support for ColBERTv2.
Simple single-step tasks; no labeled data available
Multi-step pipelines with modest data budgets
High-stakes pipelines where instruction wording matters
Specializing model weights for narrow domains
Best accuracy; combines prompt and weight optimization
Single-step instruction optimization without multi-step composability
if training examples are sourced from user-submitted text, adversarial inputs can end up as baked-in few-shot demonstrations. Filter training data before compilation.
an optimizer will find the prompt that maximizes the metric, even if that means exploiting metric blind spots. Pair automated metrics with periodic human spot-checks.
as the underlying LLM is updated by the provider, a compiled program's baked-in few-shot examples may become less effective. Schedule periodic recompilation.
compilation records every LLM input and output in the trace. If training examples contain PII, ensure traces are stored securely and purged appropriately.
set `temperature=0` during compilation evaluation to get deterministic metric scores. Use a non-zero temperature only during final inference if diversity is needed.

Think of DSPy the same way you think of a machine learning training loop. In supervised learning, you declare a model architecture, supply labeled data, define a loss function, and an optimizer finds the weights that minimize the loss. In DSPy, you declare a program architecture (Signatures and Modules), supply labeled input-output examples, define a metric function (the loss), and an optimizer finds the prompt parameters (few-shot demonstrations and instructions) that maximize the metric. The key insight is that prompt parameters are just as tunable as model weights — the difference is that optimizing them requires LLM calls rather than gradient steps. Once you internalize this analogy, the API design of DSPy becomes obvious: Signatures are forward-pass declarations, Modules are layers, Optimizers are training algorithms, and save/load is checkpointing.
DSPy separates program structure (what to compute) from prompt parameters (how to say it), and optimizes the latter automatically.
The four building blocks are Signature, Module, Optimizer, and Metric — master these before reaching for advanced optimizers.
BootstrapFewShot is the right starting point; move to MIPRO only when you have 50+ examples and instruction wording is a bottleneck.
A good metric is more important than a sophisticated optimizer; spend time designing the metric before tuning the optimizer.
Compiled programs are serializable JSON artifacts; treat them like model checkpoints in version control.
DSPy excels at multi-step pipelines; for single-step tasks with no labeled data, manual prompt engineering is still faster.
Recompile when you change the underlying LLM, add substantially more training data, or observe metric degradation in production.
